entry.title: SAP EWM Podcast - Episode 25 - SAP's new Solution SAP Logistics Management
entry.content:
Welcome to our blog post for the SAP EWM Podcast Episode 25 – SAP's new Solution SAP Logistics Management. You can listen to the podcast episode in English and in German on Spotify, Amazon Music, Apple Podcasts or Sway! Enjoy!

SAP Logistics Management: Filling the Gap Between Simplicity and Complexity

When it comes to warehouse management, SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) has long been the go-to solution for large, complex operations. From massive spare parts warehouses to production supply chains, EWM delivers the depth and sophistication needed to handle demanding logistics environments. But what about the smaller, simpler warehouses? Surprisingly, analysts estimate that nearly one-third of warehouses worldwide are still managed with paper or spreadsheets. For these businesses, EWM can feel overwhelming—too powerful for their needs, too complex for a quick rollout.

This is where SAP’s new solution comes in: SAP Logistics Management.

Why a New Solution?

SAP EWM covers complexity levels 3 to 5, where automation, industry-specific processes, and advanced services are essential. Many customers desire a simplified solution for warehouses of Levels 1 and 2 warehouses — those with basic functions and minimal automation. SAP Logistics Management is designed to offer a streamlined, accessible solution for smaller warehouses or satellite locations that don’t require the full scope of EWM.

More than just Warehouse Management!

While warehouse processes are at the heart of Logistics Management, the solution goes further:

  • Transport Management: The solution focuses on dispatching and it supports scenarios like parcel shipping, less-than-truckload (LTL & direct LTL), and no shipping (e.g. customer self-pickup for Ex-Works). Full truckload (FTL) capabilities are on the roadmap.

  • Network Integration: Logistics Management connects warehouse and transport processes into a broader logistics ecosystem, ensuring seamless coordination.

 

SAP Logistics Management combines Warehouse Logistics, Transportation Logistics and Network Logistics

This combination makes it more than just a “lightweight warehouse tool.” It’s a unified logistics platform for companies with simple needs today but ambitions to grow tomorrow.

Who Benefits?

SAP Logistics Management is tailored for:

  • Smaller businesses with straightforward logistics processes.

  • Large enterprises managing multiple small or satellite warehouses alongside their main EWM-driven hubs.

  • Companies focused on transport first, such as those replacing legacy solutions like WM/Stock Room Management or LE-TRA, with the option to add warehouse functionality later.

Completing the Portfolio

SAP already offers a reduced version of EWM in the SAP Cloud ERP , but that solution is integrated only. SAP Logistics Management expands the portfolio by providing a decentralized option that can connect to other ERP systems, including SAP Cloud ERP Private or even SAP ECC. This flexibility ensures that SAP can now support logistics operations across the full spectrum—from paper-based warehouses just starting their digital journey to highly automated global distribution centers.

 

Technical Integration and Future Capabilities

SAP Logistics Management fills the gap between complex and simple warehouses. But complexity isn’t defined only by warehouse size — it’s also about the software requirements. Logistics Management is a full-fledged Warehouse Management System (WMS): it manages storage bins, inbound and outbound processes, deliveries, inventory counts, and more. So how does the technical integration actually work?

Decentralized Architecture

SAP Logistics Management is designed as a decentralized solution. This means it connects to ERP systems via interfaces rather than being embedded directly. Key aspects include:

  • Master data integration: Materials and business partners are transferred, with batch data coming soon.

  • Transactional data: Purchase orders and deliveries are imported via APIs and transformed into “Logistical Demands”—the core documents Logistics Management operates on.

  • Future expansion: Additional ERP documents such as inbound deliveries, customer orders, stock transfers, production orders, and returns will be integrated over time.

ERP Integration Focus

  • Initial focus is on S/4HANA Private Cloud integration.

  • S/4HANA Public Cloud connectivity is planned for Q3 2026.

  • Integration runs through the SAP Integration Suite, ensuring standardized and scalable connectivity.

Functional Scope at First Release

The first general availability release in February 2026 will already cover a wide range of features. However, some capabilities will follow shortly after e.g. Batch management is planned to be available in Q1 2026.  Serial numbers are not yet supported at launch, though the inventory model is technically prepared for them. Compared to EWM or TM, Logistics Management will initially have fewer advanced features. But as a true Public Cloud product, it benefits from continuous innovation: new functionalities are delivered every two weeks, without waiting for rigid release cycles.
You can find more information in the SAP Logistics Management Product Documentation. 

AI Support with Joule

A standout feature is the integration of SAP Joule, the AI Copilot. This makes the system easier to learn and use. Users can ask natural-language questions like “Help me create a storage bin” or “Show me today’s deliveries.” No need to memorize transaction codes—the AI assistant guides users through everyday tasks

SAP Logistics Management Home Screen powered by Joule

 

Cloud Extensibility and SAP BTP Foundation

SAP Logistics Management isn’t just about simplifying warehouse operations—it’s also about embracing the Public Cloud mindset. With new interfaces, extensibility options, and a modern architecture, the solution is designed to evolve rapidly while keeping costs and complexity under control.

Extensibility in the Cloud

Flexibility is essential in logistics. SAP Logistics Management plans to offer several ways to extend functionality:

  • APIs: Starting in 2026, SAP will release APIs to enable deeper integration and customization.

  • Key-User Extensibility: This extensibility option is also planned for 2026 and will enable customers to add fields not available in the standard system for example - a familiar feature from SAP’s public cloud portfolio.

  • Label and List Customization: Layouts for loading lists and labels can already be adjusted, including adding company logos.

Unlike traditional systems, customers no longer access database tables directly (no SE16 or manual SELECT statements). Instead, extensions are handled through APIs, ensuring a Clean Core. This approach makes the bi-weekly updates possible without disrupting customer systems.

Built on SAP BTP

SAP Logistics Management is developed on the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), similar to other new solutions like Digital Manufacturing (DM). This has several advantages:

  • Lightweight provisioning: Setting up a Logistics Management instance takes minutes, not hours.

  • Cost efficiency: Running outside the full S/4HANA stack reduces overhead.

  • Continuous innovation: New features are delivered every two weeks. They remain inactive until customers choose to enable them which ensures stable operations of the solution. This model ensures customers benefit from rapid innovation without the burden of constant regression testing.

SAP Logistics Management is not the successor of SAP EWM or SAP TM

A common question is whether SAP Logistics Management replaces EWM (Extended Warehouse Management) or TM (Transportation Management). The answer is clear: No. 
Logistics Management focuses on simpler warehouses and transport scenarios, not large distribution centers or multimodal transport networks. SAP EWM and SAP TM continue to evolve. The recent S/4HANA 2025 release introduced new features, and the roadmap is full of enhancements for future releases. 

SAP’s strategy is complementary: SAP Logistics Management fills the gap for smaller, decentralized operations, while SAP EWM and SAP TM remain the backbone for complex, large-scale logistics.

A New Path for Warehousing and Transport

For retail customers with large distribution centers and complex material flow systems (MFS), the choice is clear: SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM). Likewise, when transport requirements involve multiple modes of transportation or advanced features like route optimization and driver planning, the answer is equally straightforward: SAP Transportation Management (TM).

But many of these same customers also operate smaller warehouses or have simpler transport needs. In these cases, the new SAP Logistics Management (LGM) solution may be the perfect fit.

Imagine a “pop-up” warehouse rented temporarily to handle seasonal demand or additional capacity. With Logistics Management, such a site can be up and running quickly, without the complexity of a full EWM implementation.

First Impressions of SAP Logistics Management

Set Up the solution

Manage your warehouse layout

Manage your day with the help of Joule

SAP Logistics Management Home Screen powered by SAP Joule

 

Run the solution 

Manage Transports and Consignments

 Manage outbound deliveries

Mobile Working with SAP Logistics Management

 Packing at Packing Station with SAP Logistics Management

 Key Takeaways

From SAP’s perspective, the most important points about Logistics Management are:

  • SAP Logistics Management does not replace SAP EWM and SAP TM.

  • Fast and cost-effective implementation is a core strength.

  • Modern user interfaces with full Joule/AI support make the system intuitive.

  • Continuous innovation ensures new features are delivered regularly.

A New Journey in the Public Cloud

SAP Logistics Management marks the beginning of a new journey for warehouse and transport solutions. Every journey starts with a first step—and that step has now been taken. 

With its focus on simplicity, speed, and innovation, Logistics Management promises to be an exciting and successful addition to SAP’s logistics portfolio, complementing SAP EWM and  SAP TM rather than replacing them.

 
Please visit our SAP EWM Podcast Landing Page with all Episodes, Blog Posts and Podcast Notes!

entry.title: SAP EWM RF Exception Handling - Deep Dive with BIDP- Partial Pick denial example
entry.content:
Business Scenario:

Let's set a quick business scenario, we have an outbound delivery order for a customer , where we need to pick 8 EA of the product from the warehouse. Say, we have Bin A and Bin B which have the stock for the product we are trying to pick.  The warehouse task is created to pick everything from Bin A, but while picking the picker using RF, encountered issue like only 2 EA are ok and rest are damaged, therefore, the picker will enter the exception code in EWM RF - e.g. BIDP ( Partial Pick denial) and system will then search  an alternate bin to pick the remaining 6 EA.

Example figure:

Source: Ai Generated


Note:   We can block  the Source Bin A also with the exception  handling  configuration  settings  with standard workflow  to  avoid picking the damaged stock or creation of WT's for any new orders. But this is not the scope of this blog.

RF Execution and Debugging:

1. We have an open WO to be picked, we use  'Selection  by WO' or 'selection by queue' or system guided  options

Created the  Pick HU and click F4 Next

2. Operator entered BIDP exception


Then operator hit Enter,

3. This will go the below perform  bind_check_and_initiate under FM- /SCWM/RF_PICK_EXCEPTION

It updates the BIND field which is the key parameter to  go for bin  denial 

In case of IV_IPRCODE = BIDP  which is  the  entered exception  code, it updates the BIND field in CS_ORDIM_CONFIRM-BIND

It then modify the ct_ordim_confirm

4. The control goes back to the DIFFBD function code. Which brings new screen to enter the actual quantity and the difference. then operator presses F1 Save.

The actual picking quantity is updated with 2 EA

Scan and confirm the Warehouse task.

Note:

I have used skip verification field  exception to skip scanning the source HU field . Therefore you may see **** in exception field in WT.

5. It goes to the class- /SCWM/CL_RF_CONFIRM_PICKING method CONFIRM where it calls the CREATE_BIN_DENIAL method.

This is the main class.

Here it tries to create new WT for the remaining quantity which is 6EA in this example, 

You can notice it will only pass the  IF statement  if the IS_ORDIM_CONFIRM-BIND not  initial, which is coming from above step 2.

Note:  The bin denial  scenarios is only considered for the below document categories.

6. Trying to find the source bin excluding the current bin with the remaining required quantity via FM  /SCWM/REM_BIN_DET

BAPIRET messages - Here we can see it found an alternate bin to pick the remaining quantity.

 

7. Then the information is passed to another FM /SCWM/TO_PREP_WHR_UI_INT to create the WT against the WHR

Important comment :

It tries to add into same Warehouse order by running simulations.

8. It returns to the picking screen again to  pick the remaining quantity within the same WO.

WT  status, bin to resource is confirmed

a. WT 9007928 is the original product WT with original pick quantity as 8 EA ( 2 EA actual pick + 6 EA Difference). Updated  with the below exceptions.

b. WT 9007932 is the new bin denial task to pick remaining 6 EA from different bin. Continue to  pick from this, note I have used SKFD in the new WT also.

Finally the  destination screen is shown-

Final WO status - confirmed.

END.

This concludes the blog. To sum up, we have seen  some insights with debug mode how the backend logic of BIDP exception works with the important class, method and function modules.

Thanks for reading. Please like and share your valuable comments.

 

Best Regards,

Y K B B Rudra Narayan

entry.title: Route Train operation for Inbound processes
entry.content:
With SAP S/4HANA for Manufacturing logistics 2310 FPS01 we have enhanced the list of processes which are covered by route train with the following entities:

  • Inbound deliveries
  • Warehouse task without Warehouse request
  • Outbound deliveries for Plant maintenance orders

The following blog will describe the required setup for an inbound delivery-related route train scenario.

Background

Inbound processes are supported with SAP S/4HANA for Manufacturing Logistics in 2 ways:

  • Inbound deliveries from Goods receipt from Production
  • Inbound deliveries from external vendors

Inbound deliveries from Production support the putaway of HUs from a production line back to the warehouse while inbound deliveries from external vendors either supply the production itself or can be used to putaway received HUs into the warehouse.

Goods receipt from Production

Production processes need to be set up in advance and we use in this scenario a manufacturing order, which has been created upfront. 

You can e.g. post receipt of HUs based on a manufacturing order. The receipt of Hus can be performed e.g. via RF in SAP EWM with the logical transaction for Receiving of HUs by Manufacturing Order..

You logon to a suitable workcenter and then enter the manufacturing order.

Figure 1: Enter your Manufacturing Order in RF

With the manufacturing order input you need to verify the produced product. When you create an HU, the system automatically creates an inbound delivery for the HU.

Figure 2: Create a new HU in RF 

Figure 3: Created inbound delivery with determined route on item level

This inbound delivery then determines automatically a route based on the condition records, in this scenario we use the destination bin of the HU as decisive criterion for the route determination

The destination bin determination in inbound processes usually happens late in the process-oriented storage control. SAP S/4 HANA for Manufacturing Logistics however needs to determine the destination bin early in the process so that the route train process already knows at the beginning of the document flow where the HU will end up. This information of the final destination for putaway will be determined based on a PPF action /ILO/PDI_01_HU_UPDATE. It determines the destination bin and calls the logistical revaluation to determine the route based on the destination bin. Logistical revaluation then updates the route on inbound delivery item level and HU level.   

After the route has been determined the process for the route train does not differ from the already known loading and unloading process for production supply processes:

Figure 4: Warehouse task with route for inbound delivery

You load your HU and create a tour.

Figure 5: Load HU in RF onto your towing vehicle 

You start the unloading process by departing from the start point

Figure 6: Start unloading process

You unload your HUs at the destination bins and complete your tour.

Figure 7: Unload HU to final destination bin in storage type T040

With this you have loaded HUs from production and brought them back the warehouse for putaway.  More details can be foound on the official help page. 

Goods receipt from external vendors

A similar process exists for inbound deliveries from external vendors. SAP S/4HANA for Manufacturing Logistics supports inbound deliveries with a TM scenario using Inbound Cargo registration and inbound deliveries without TM integration. Both processes share the same concept for the route determination: the determination procedure is assigned to the document type and suitable condition records are created.

Inbound deliveries from ICR usually have subHUs in topHUs. The system determines a route for each subHU in the topHU. In case not all determined routes for all subHUs are identical you must manually repack the subHUs which do not fit to the determined route of the remaining HUs. 

For the route train process from external vendors the inbound delivery must be created e.g. manually or via ASN inbound processing. The route determination for an ICR-related delivery starts with the update of the status “ready for warehouse processing”. This triggers as usual the route determination.

The system then creates warehouse tasks for staging and loading. The HUs are loaded onto a tour and are then brought to the PSA or destination bin using the already well-known route train applications in RF.  

One important detail needs to be considered for the demand time calculation: when a HU has a reference to a JIT call and the JIT call has a demand time, then this time is used as the planned arrival time of the tour in case you work with tacted tours. The tour determination then tries to find a suitable tour, which has a end time before the planned demand time at the PSA so that the goods arrive in time at the PSA.

Required configuration

In order to determine a route for inbound deliveries, you need to configure a route determination procedure for your document type of the relevant inbound deliveries.

The stop concept of SAP S/4HANA for manufacturing logistics has been enhanced with release 2023 FPS01. While we supported before this release only PSAs as stops we now also support usual storage bins as destinations. You need to maintain the bins as stops in the Manage Stops app and can then use them in your route for planning and in your condition records for the determination of the route.

entry.title: Route Train support process: pickup of HUs after unloading has started
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA for Manufacturing Logistics, route trains usually bring goods from the warehouse to destinations (usually production supply areas) in the warehouse where they are e.g. consumed and put into new products.

The supply process can start with a variety of warehouse request document categories like e.g. WMRs for JIT, Kanban with WHR or inbound deliveries. 

We supported up to 2310 FPS01 the additional loading of an HU before the unloading has started. But when the unloading had started it was not possible to add additional HUs onto the tour.

The support of cleanup processes like Clear PSA or on-demand pickup of HUs from PSAs was not supported until SAP S/4HANA for Manufacturing Logistics 2310 FPS01.

With this release we support the pickup of HUs on the stops of a route with a variety of process variants:

  • HU with loading warehouse task to endpoint of the route
  • HU without loading warehouse task to endpoint of the route
  • HU with loading warehouse task for putaway beyond endpoint of the route
  • HU without loading warehouse task for putaway beyond endpoint of the route

The HU can either be a structured HU (aka rack) or a usual HU, it can contain products or other HUs or it can be empty.  This gives the users a lot of freedom in the logistics processes. 

Preconditions

The HU which shall be moved back to the warehouse must meet the following requirements:

  • There must be no route assigned to the HU
  • There is no reference document in the HU item available

You can maintain the destination bin e.g. manually in the EWM warehouse monitor under e.g. Documents – Handling Units and then chose the method “Change HU Destination Data”.

If the destination is not part of the current route then the system assumes a multi-step processing from the current bin of the HU to the final bin:

  • movement to the endpoint of the route
  • movement from there with standard EWM routing to the destination.

To achieve the warehouse task to the final destination ,the system determines the way to the endpoint using the putaway strategy to get the correct warehouse process type and possible intermediate steps. 

 

Process description

The operator is on a tour for unloading HUs to stops and unloads HUs. This means that the status of the tour is already “Unloading started”.

After the first HUs have been unloaded the operator can pickup an HU which is at a stop of the current tour.  The operator choses the function code CF1 for Pick up HUs.

Figure 1: Pickup in RF 

The operator enters the HU ID of the picked-up HU. As the HU is not a rack the system requests a destination HU; the operator scans one of the existing racks. The system creates an unloading warehouse task for the picked-up HU to the destination bin of the route.

Figure 2: Pickup in RF: Enter source HU 

Figure 3: Pickup in RF: Enter destination HU 

The operator then continues the unloading process and returns to the endpoint of the route. The confirmation of the endpoint of the route also confirms the unloading warehouse task for the picked-up HU and posts all empties to the endpoint of the route. 

Conclusion

From an operation perspective this scenario can be enhanced with additional warehouse tasks from the endpoint of the route to e.g. an empties buffer or to another staging area or picking process.

It finally gives the users the possibility to have an overview about empty HUs in the supply process; this was not available so far. 

More details can be found here.

entry.title: EWM QM Serial Number Integration on S4 HANA 2025 FPS00
entry.content:
Business Problem Prior to S4 HANA 2025- At goods receipt no serial number transfer to inspection lot. Instead at inspection result recording serial numbers need to be recorded manually (transaction QE51N) which is not available for version lesser than < S4 HANA 2025.

New Functionality from S4 HANA 2025 FPS00- This New feature enables to record a quality inspection result for a specific serialized product in the Quality Management (QM) component of SAP S/4HANA by recording a usage decision for the serialized product with reference to its serial number. The system copies the serial number to the inspection lot in the QM component during the goods receipt posting of the stock item.

Main Features of QM EWM Serial number Integration.

  • Valid for both Embedded and Decentral systems.
  •  Used in IOT4 and IOT5 scenarios (both Ad-Hoc and Recurring) as well as in Synchronous Posting.
  • Summary Level Inspection can happen prior to UD( Technically at time of Result recording)
  • During results recording,  Inspection results for single products with reference to their corresponding serial numbers can be recorded( Summary Level).
  • Summary level can only be invoked for Inbound delivery posting and hence not possible for Synchronous Posting.

How to Set up-

Activate serial Number integration in IOT 4 or 5 customizing( The summary level QM EWM is implicitly explained in F4)

 

Configure Serial Number Profile

Use the Procedure QMSL( Usage must be 3)

 

Master Data MM03

Activate Inspection Type 01( Embedded EWM) & 1701 in Case of Decentralized EWM on S4 with below indicator( Serial Numbers Possible for Inspection Lot)

SAP implicitly mentioning 3 things here

  1. 'Inspection Lot Summary' can be conducted even with Serial no activation 
  2. Serial number can be copied to Pre-sampling in Production scenarios
  3. Serial number is copied during GR posting( IOT 4 & IOT 5), Inbound delivery based as well Synchronous posting based.

Testing performed using MIGO( Synchronous Based)

Serial numbers are assigned to Inspection Lot QA03

Serial numbers are available at time of Result Recording QE01/QE51N( Screen shot of GR posted of 3 EA of different serial numbers than above)

 

Serial number are available at time of Usage decision( This functionality was already available from S4H 1709 onwards & 9.5)

This functionality of QM EWM Serial number integration is now exactly same like IM Based QM integration which earlier was missing.

Thanks for reading. All tests are performed in S4 HANA 2025 FPS00 Sandbox system 

References:

Help. SAP Serial Numbers in Inspection Results | SAP Help Portal

SAP Extended Warehouse Management for SAP S/4HANA ... - SAP Community

entry.title: Mapping Stock to New Nested Handling Units Using Migration Cockpit in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud
entry.content:
Mapping Stock to New Nested Handling Units Using Migration Cockpit in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

Step 1: Go to the ‘Migrate Your Data’ tile and create a project by selecting the ‘HUM – Handling Unit’ object.

Img:1

Img:2

Img:3

Img:4

Step 2: Once the project is created, select the project and download the migration template.

 

Step 3: Fill in the required data in the tabs of the template. The detailed steps are provided below.

Tab 1: Bundles for Nested HUs

  • Maintain a Run ID when creating Nested HUs for a material.
  • The Run ID is used to group multiple Handling Units into a single Nested HU structure.

Example: If a carton containing material is packed together with other cartons onto a pallet, the cartons (inner HUs) and the pallet (outer HU) together form a Nested Handling Unit structure and must share the same Run ID (Bundle ID).

Tab 2: Header Data

  • Since an external number range is maintained in the backend configuration for HU packing types, the External HU ID is filled for the respective packing materials, Plant details.

Tab 3: Item Data

  • Maintain the material against the respective HU ID and enter the quantity to be migrated into the new Handling Units.

Scenario example used in this document:

  • At Pallet HU level, the Carton Box HU is assigned.
  • At Carton Box HU level, the Small Box HU is assigned.
  • At the Small Box HU level, the material “42 – SFG Material” is maintained with a quantity of 15 KG.

When this data is uploaded, the system:

  • Automatically assigns the stock to the respective HUs, and Creates the complete Nested HU structure, finally resulting in the Pallet HU containing all lower-level HUs.

Step 4: Upload File and Mapping Task

  • Upload the completed data template.

 

Step 5: Simulate

Step 6: Migrate

  • Finally, migration completed, the system creates the Handling Units (HUs) and assigns them to the existing stock for the material.

Check Stock: Display Stock Overview

  • The below screenshot confirms successful creation of Nested HUs, with stock correctly assigned from Small Box → Carton Box → Pallet HU.

  • Using Migration Cockpit, we successfully mapped existing stock into a nested HU structure, enabling accurate HU tracking and improved stock visibility in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

entry.title: Technical Deep dive to understand HU label print from Work Center with PPF actions with scenarios
entry.content:
1. Introduction:

This blog will give you an idea & help you to understand what standard coding is trying to perform when you hit the print HU button in the work center. How the Action merging and processing time influence the PPF action processing and also the main tips and tricks to find the condition record is matching or not where we often find that sometimes even though the condition record is there system is unable to print. PPF topic has been always been technical in nature so this blog tries to decode the mystery.

Hope you will enjoy reading this blog  and it will be helpful in your projects.

Spoiler Alert : This blog is going to be a long one where i explained different scenarios viz.,

             a. First time HU label  print b. 2nd time print with no un-processed PPF c. Printing with an un-processed PPF entry

             Have patience and kindly go through. Hope it will  help  you to get the full E2E logic. 

 Disclaimer :

The process flow image is SAP Proprietary and adapted from the how to guide to understand  and explain the backend  logic https://d.dam.sap.com/a/jSy4Xih/PPF_in_SAP_EWM_2021.pdf?rc=10

2. Processing algorithm :

Taking the reference from above flow diagram, I will break down the algorithm into below main parts:

a. Trigger of HU Print from  Work-center

b. Fetching the active action definitions with Action merging class from config

c. Schedule condition determination

  • Understanding the LO_CONTEXT object. 
  • Understanding the LT_PRINT_CON object.  
  • Gets the print determination procedure
  • Checks the context values against the relevant DB condition record table
  • It builds the internal table with Action definition and then tries to find the valid condition record against the action definition
  • Once the valid record is found, it updates the RES_VALID field with ‘X’ or 'Y' and add a dummy record with RES_VALID = ‘’ value

d. Set trigger data with some given BADI to influence the trigger values

e. Action Merging logic- Compares old PPF trigger vs New PPF trigger and decide based on the different cases

In this scenario, I have used-

EWM: Max. 1 Unproc. Actn for an Actn Def., Do not Del + lock   This is the recommended action merging from SAP.

For more information, you can check the below links and SAP  NOTE:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SUPPORT_CONTENT/sewm/4158330038.html

https://me.sap.com/notes/1393635/E  (1393635 - Consulting: PPF Merge Logic usage within EWM Delivery)

3. Action Definition and Condition Record:

All the testing and below scenario is based on Action definition HU_CONTENT_GENERAL_AND_RF under Action Profile /SCWM/HU.

Condition Record maintained in /SCWM/PRHU6 transaction.

4. TESTING

Scenario 1 : First time HU label  print 

1. Trigger the HU print from Work center

2. In this scenario, as mentioned above I am using the action definition HU_CONTENT_GENERAL_AND_RF but in the standard  configuration generally we have many active action definitions, therefore to reduce the number of iterations you can delete the unwanted action  definitions in debugging mode shown below. 😇 ( Tip)

Class -  CL_CONTEXT_MANAGER_PPF

Method- CONSTRUCTOR

Double click on object value as  shown below, it will show the data entries and if you open the details  you will see the action definitions. We only kept our one.

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

3. NOTE: The Class- CL_TTYPE_PPF and method DETERMINE is the important one where the Scheduling condition determination, Action Merging , setting of old and new trigger and execution of PPF happens. We will see it in detail.

4. Schedule condition determination:

The Function  module   /SCWM/SCHEDULE_COND_HU is having the main schedule logic for  HU.

4a. In this FM, the 1st important aspect  is the LO_CONTEXT object.  This object is having the details of the Warehouse, HU, Packaging material etc details. These details will be later used to compare and read the correct condition records.

4b. Another important object is LT_PRINT_CON. This is set if any active condition record is found in previous condition determination. This is the first time print so it is blank. This will be useful in scenario 2 and 3.

4c. Determine the Determination procedure. Here it uses the LO_COTEXT->CALLER  value. In this case it is '2' trigger from work center, if not found then it will search '1' in cases where the HU print is from e.g. monitor. It gets 0HUPR determination procedure from work center.

4d. Condition record determination - Here it checks the valid condition records based on the configuration of condition type, access sequence, condition table etc setup and compares with the context determined in step 4a.

Note :In this we can find the view /1CN/APHSAPHU002 via we can get the DB table /1CN/CPHSAPHU002 where the condition is stored which program is trying to compare with. Similarly we have standard table /1CN/CPHSAPHU00 to /1CN/CPHSAPHU004 for  Print HU.

Double click to see the  details  of the records the system is comparing. Example.

LT_WHERE it gets the  details from condition table. 

We can see the details and then it selects the record with respect to the CONTEXT value.

SY-SUBRC = 0 ,means the condition record is satisfied.

4e. Build internal table LT_PRINT_CON with the found valid conditions and match with the action definition.

4f. It found the valid record, it then updates the RES_VALID with 'X' into  LT_GOOD_CON table. Updates the log that matched record found.

Note: Important  logic mentioned here  for  re-print. Where it states the action scheduling will not happen.

Next, it checks if there is valid condition or not. If found, then it adds a dummy entry with RES_VALID  as space in LT_PRINT_CON & updates persistent class object  value. This will be fetched every time if we  try to trigger another print for the same HU after the first schedule condition determination gets successful.

So, in this case we get 2 entries as shown below.

Finally the EP_RC =0 and schedule condition is successful.

5. Set Trigger.

The control again comes back to class  CL_TTYPE_PPF and method DETERMINE as shown in step 3.

Note: Badi is given to influence the trigger.

It then get the old and new PPF triggers

6.  Action Merging. 

Based on the old and new triggers, merging logic decides whether to keep the new trigger or delete.

In this scenario, it didn't find any old PPF trigger as it is the first time we are printing the HU label.

It updates the new trigger, and passes it for execution.

In the  execution logic, it checks the status of the new trigger is 0 , means  un-processed and the processing  as '3' which is immediate processing. If it is  not matched then  it will be in  amber to be manually processed via SPPFP transaction code.

The processing time is 4 in our case, so the PPF is not immediately processed.

7. Application log, SPPFP and Print output.

Scenario 2 : 2nd time print with no un-processed PPF

In this scenario we will see the data saved in persistent class object in scenario 1 step 4f.

1.  LO_CONTEXT object. Here we can see the T_PRINT_CON is already filled.

2. Like Scenario 1, the condition record is again found.

3. The new found condition record is added to LT_PRINT_CON to  make it  2 records. one  is already coming from persistent class having RES_VALID  = 'X' and the new one with RES_VALID = ''

4. Searching the new record with RES_VALID as space  and checking against the PPF action definition. Updates 'X' to RES_VALID and append to LT_GOOD_CON for the new condition record.

Then it checks LT_PRINT_CON where we have 2 records, and update RES_VALID as 'Y' for the old found record

Finally the schedule condition is successful with EP_RC= 0

5. In the Action merging logic, it gets 2 trigger records. one is new and one is old.

It keeps the new trigger as there are no un-processed old trigger.

Status 1 means PPF trigger is processed. this is the old ppf trigger which we did in Scenario 1.

The new one is having status 0, un-processed

6. PPF action triggered and it  is in  amber  status in SPPFP.

Scenario 3 : Printing with an un-processed PPF entry

1. This time we  trigger  the print keep the un-processed PPF  determined from  Scenario 2.

2. It checks the triggers, where it gets the old triggers and new triggers.

This is the 1st old trigger with status =1 

Which is still processed entry

Checking the another old trigger, this the new one with status = 0. This is the un-processed entry

Thereby, it gets and un-processed trigger & compares it  with the new trigger.

Based on the Action  merging selected criteria it will delete the new action  if  any un-processed trigger is there.

Finally the new action details  are deleted.

3.  Application  log

4. CONCLUSION: 

This is the end of the blog. It shows the detail logic during different scenarios while printing HU label, how the schedule condition logic works, condition determination, get new and old triggers and finally the action merging logic.

Thanks for reading. Please like and share your valuable comments.

Best Regards,

Y K B B Rudra Narayan

entry.title: Backflush program based on Plant with Sequential Sorting per Order- Enhanced Feature from S4H 2023
entry.content:
New Functionality-From Release S4CORE 108( can be downported to lower version), SAP has enhanced the CORUPROC( Backflush Program) and has added below fields to execute the Program based on actual business needs.

  • AUFNR( Order Number)
  • WERKS( Plant)
  • Sort Order by Order Number 

Business requirement-  For a customer,  We had built a custom Fiori App which is based on synchronous GR and GI posting on Process Orders confirmation. Since the consumption, GR and confirmation are triggered by  BAPI_PROCORDCONF_CREATE_TT and APIs , the immediate GR and GI sometime end up in locking issue.  

To handle locking issue for immediate GR/ Backflush/ Confirmation, SAP generally recommends to decouple the GR and GI process if these are handled via BAPI/APIs. In nutshell, GR and confirmation were handled on real time basis but Backflush posting are posted via background job to avoid HU/Batch consumption because components are cross orders which sometime ends up in locking.( Parallel Posting of HU ends up in COGI if backflush is online driven)

In this case, SAP has given Option to Post GR( real time) and Consumption( In background) to avoid locking issue. Also SAP suggest to run ‘CORUPROC’ program in background to park confirmation and trigger ‘BACKFLUSH’ via Job. GR and confirmation are handled in real time via below config & Backflush using  job( SE38 CORUPROC)

Earlier CORUPROC program has no AUFNR & WERKS field which lacked Plant based Backflush posting, however with this enhanced note Backflush can be executed per Plant and consumption of Components order can be synchronized with Order based posting( One by one)

With a modification note( 1008686 - CORUPROC: Selection by order and plant),  its now possible  to use the order number and plant as additional selection criteria. Also a new  parameter P_SORT (Sorting by Order Number only for S4HANA 2023). If the parameter is set, the goods movements are sorted by order number in advance. This Sorting parameter of in CORUPROC always consume order based on staging of Raw materials which is better aligned to customer backflush/consumption strategy. This parameter also improves the performance if storage locations are EWM-relevant and  production material requests are used during staging.

To use this functionality, the confirmation parameter is adjusted to immediate facilitate GR and confirmation. However, the backflush of components to be posted via Background job( CORUPROC)

Post note implementation( Its a modification Note), it introduces additional fields and sorting parameter

Scheduled the job of the CORUPROC program 

  The job is scheduled to consume in every 2 minutes Per Plant and Per Order

Created 3 Order( PMR based Cross Order) and confirmation is posted 

1000642, 1000643 and  1000644.

Sequentially posted the confirmation of 3 Orders and CORUPROC job( Per Plant/ Order) was scheduled.

The CORUPROC job was executed and consumed the Order components sequentially

time stamp of backflush sequentially.

PMR updation.

Thanks for reading. All tests are performed in S4 HANA 2025 FPS00 version.

Reference-  

  • 1008686 - CORUPROC: Selection by order and plant
  • 208477 - Consulting: Process control in confirmation

This functionality will only work on Embedded EWM. Never use backflush from S4 if its Decentralized EWM on S4H. Customer should always built a custom program or logic using /SCWM/MFG_CONSUME_ITEMS_EXT if its decentral EWM.

entry.title: SAP EWM Product Master Upload Best Practices
entry.content:
SAP EWM Product Master Upload Best Practices

Introduction

Product master data forms the foundation of all warehouse operations in SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM). Proper management of this critical master data directly impacts warehouse efficiency, inventory accuracy, and overall operational performance. SAP Note 3302269 addresses common challenges organizations face when uploading product master data, particularly around required fields in CSV templates and handling empty fields during the upload process.

Understanding and implementing best practices for product master uploads is essential for successful EWM implementations, whether you're migrating from legacy systems, performing regular maintenance, or managing warehouse go-lives. Poor data quality or incorrect upload procedures can lead to operational disruptions, process failures, and significant business impact.

Understanding EWM Product Master Data

EWM product master data consists of multiple layers of information that control how materials behave within warehouse operations. The key components include:

Global Product Data: Basic material information transferred from ERP systems (MM01/MM02/MM03 transactions) stored in tables like MARA, MARC, and MARD. This includes material numbers, descriptions, base units of measure, and general material properties .

Warehouse-Specific Data: EWM-specific attributes stored in table /SAPAPO/MATLWH with key fields PRODUCT, LGNUM (warehouse number), and ENTITLED (party entitled to dispose). This includes put-away control indicators, stock removal indicators, and warehouse-specific processing rules ,.

Storage Type Dependent Data: Detailed storage specifications stored in /SAPAPO/MATLWHST, including storage type assignments, fixed bin indicators, and storage-specific parameters ,.

The relationship between ECC/S4HANA and EWM is hierarchical - basic product views must exist in the ERP system before warehouse-specific extensions can be created in EWM ,.

Pre-Upload Preparation

Data Quality Assessment

Before initiating any upload process, conduct thorough data validation:

  • Mandatory Field Verification: Ensure all required fields are populated, particularly LGNUM (warehouse number) and ENTITLED (party entitled to dispose) for warehouse data 
  • LPTYP Field Validation: When the LPTYP (storage type) field is empty in templates, related fields like maximum and minimum quantities may be cleared during upload
  • Business Rule Consistency: Validate that put-away control indicators, stock removal indicators, and other business rules align with warehouse operational requirements 

System Prerequisites

Verify system readiness before upload execution:

  • ERP Product Existence: Confirm that basic product master data exists in MM01/MM02/MM03 transactions in the source ERP system 
  • Integration Configuration: Validate that all referenced master data (storage types, packaging materials, transportation groups) exists in both ERP and EWM systems 
  • Authorization Setup: Ensure users have appropriate authorizations for product master maintenance and mass upload transactions 

Best Practice Upload Methods

Method 1: Warehouse Monitor Mass Creation

The EWM Warehouse Monitor provides an intuitive interface for mass product master creation and maintenance :

Step-by-Step Process:

  1. Navigate to Warehouse Monitor (/SCWM/MON) → Product Master Data → Warehouse Attributes
  2. Select "Only those products without Warehouse Data" to identify products requiring extension
  3. Select target products and choose "Mass Change/Create" from "More Methods" option
  4. Enter warehouse-specific data in the popup dialog
  5. Confirm the mass update operation

Advantages: User-friendly interface, real-time validation, immediate feedback on data quality issues Limitations: Best suited for smaller datasets, requires manual data entry Recommended Scenarios: Initial warehouse setup, ad-hoc corrections, small-scale maintenance activities 

Method 2: /SCWM/MIG_PRODUCT Transaction

This transaction enables batch processing using external CSV files :

Implementation Details:

  1. Download the SAP-provided template "SCWM MIGR fields.xlsx" from SAP Note 2602348
  2. Populate the template with product master data and save as CSV format
  3. Execute transaction /SCWM/MIG_PRODUCT
  4. Select "Upload to EWM" and "Product Master Data" options
  5. Specify warehouse number and party entitled to dispose
  6. Upload the CSV file with proper field separator (comma) configuration

Performance Considerations: Handles large datasets efficiently, supports automated processing Use Case Recommendations: Mass migrations, regular bulk updates, integration with external systems ,

Method 3: Fiori Mass Maintenance Application

For S/4HANA 2023 and later versions, the Fiori-based mass maintenance application provides modern user experience ,:

Key Features:

  • Guided process flow through Start → Scope → Selection → Edit → Validation → Activation steps
  • Built-in validation and error handling
  • Export capabilities for verification
  • Integration with Master Data Governance (MDG) framework

Business Role Requirements: SAP_BR_PRODMASTER_SPECIALIST or SAP_MD_MDC_ADMIN_APP_06 

Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them

  1. Empty LPTYP Field Issues
    • Description: When storage type (LPTYP) is empty, related quantity fields get cleared during upload 
    • Prevention Strategy: Always validate storage type assignments before uploading, separate warehouse data from storage type dependent data uploads
  2. Field Separator Configuration
    • Description: Incorrect field separator settings cause CSV parsing failures 
    • Prevention Strategy: Always set field separator to comma (,) in /SCWM/MIG_PRODUCT transaction
  3. Missing Prerequisites
    • Description: Attempting to upload warehouse data before basic ERP product exists 
    • Prevention Strategy: Verify ERP product master existence using /SAPAPO/MAT1 before warehouse extension

Performance Optimization Tips

  • Batch Size Management: Process uploads in manageable chunks (typically 1000-5000 records) to avoid system timeouts 
  • Timing Considerations: Schedule large uploads during off-peak hours to minimize system impact
  • Resource Monitoring: Monitor system resources during upload processes, particularly memory and database connections

Validation and Testing

Pre-Production Testing

Establish comprehensive testing procedures:

  • Test Data Preparation: Create representative test datasets covering all product types and scenarios
  • Validation Scenarios: Test both successful uploads and error conditions
  • Performance Benchmarking: Measure upload times and system resource consumption

Post-Upload Verification

Implement systematic verification processes:

  • Data Integrity Checks: Verify data accuracy using warehouse monitor and transaction /SCWM/MAT1 ,
  • Process Flow Validation: Test end-to-end warehouse processes with uploaded products
  • Error Log Analysis: Review upload logs for warnings or partial failures

Troubleshooting Common Issues

Issue

Symptoms

Resolution

CSV Parsing Errors

Upload fails with format errors

Verify field separator settings and CSV structure 

Missing Storage Type Data

Quantity fields cleared after upload

Separate warehouse and storage type uploads, validate LPTYP field 

Authorization Failures

Access denied during upload

Verify user roles and product master authorizations 

Integration Errors

Referenced master data not found

Ensure all dependent master data exists in both systems 

Monitoring and Maintenance

Establish ongoing governance processes:

  • Regular Data Quality Audits: Implement periodic checks for data consistency and completeness
  • Change Management: Define procedures for handling product master changes and updates
  • Performance Monitoring: Track upload performance metrics and optimize as needed

Conclusion

Successful EWM product master uploads require careful planning, proper tool selection, and adherence to established best practices. The choice between warehouse monitor, /SCWM/MIG_PRODUCT, or Fiori applications depends on your specific requirements, data volumes, and system landscape.

Key success factors include thorough pre-upload preparation, understanding the hierarchical relationship between ERP and EWM data, and implementing robust validation procedures. By following these best practices and avoiding common pitfalls, organizations can ensure reliable, efficient product master data management that supports optimal warehouse operations.

Additional Resources

  • SAP Note 3302269: EWM: Best Practices to Upload Product Master ,
  • SAP Note 2602348: Tools for Batch Warehouse Product Maintenance in S/4HANA embedded EWM ,
  • SAP Note 2627825: Mass Creation of Warehouse Products from Monitor,

 Hope that can help you.

Thank you and regards

Rogerio Lima.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Embedded EWM Putaway Strategy – Step-by-Step with Real Business Scenario
entry.content:
Putaway in SAP EWM is not just about moving stock from GR Area to a storage bin. It is about speed, optimal space utilization, automation, and accuracy.

A well designed Putaway Strategy ensures that inbound materials are:

  • Automatically directed to the correct storage type
  • Placed in the right bin without manual decisions
  • Available immediately for production or consumption

In this blog, I"ll walk you through a real business scenario explaining how 25 Pieces or raw material are received and automatically put away into Raw Material Storage Type using SAP S4HANA Embedded EWM.

🔄End-to-End Process Flow

  1. Vendor send raw materials → Purchas Order Created
  2. Inbound Delivery generated from PO
  3. Goods Receipt posted in GR Area (9010 - GR Zone)
  4. Warehouse Task Created (Storage type determined as per putaway strategy)
  5. Operator confirms Warehouse Task
  6. Stock becomes available for consumption

📌Pre-Requisite Configuration

Before executing the putaway process, ensure ERP storage locations are mapped to EWM.

Path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Interfaces → ERP Integration → Goods Movements → Map Storage Locations from ERP System to EWM

Putaway Strategy Configuration

Path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Strategies → Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

Path : SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Strategies → Assign Storage Types to Storage Type Search Sequence

Path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Strategies → Define Putaway Control Indicator

(This Put away control indicator needs to be assigned in warehouse product master data)

Path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Strategies → Specify Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Goods Receipt Process → Strategies → Optimize Access Strategy for Storage type Search

Path: SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Warehouse Task → Define Warehouse Process Type

(All warehouse process types are further grouped into warehouse process categories. Warehouse in to warehouse process categories. Warehouse process categories are used to define the goods movement in a warehouse.

There are different warehouse process categories that are predefined in the EWM system.

  1. Stock Removal
  2. Put-Away
  3. Internal Movement
  4. Goods Receipts
  5. Goods Issues)

Here the source storage type I used is 9010 and bin as GR-ZONE. So that when ever the Inbound Delivery created in EWM the material will be first received in bin GR-ZONE. Also the WH process category I used is 1 - Putaway.

Create Storage Bin (Transaction: /N/SCWM/LS01)

🧪 Execution Step-by-Step Process

Step 1: Create Material Master Record in ERP System and Product Master Record in EWM (ERP Transaction: MM01/EWM Transaction: /N/SCWM/MAT1)

EWM Transaction: /N/SCWM/MAT1

Assign Putaway Control indicator in warehouse data

Step 2: Create Purchase Order (Transaction: ME21N)

Maintain Confirmation Control: 0004 - Inbound Delivery

Check Stock post PO Creation

Step 3: Create Inbound Delivery (Transaction: VL31N)

Inbound Delivery is successfully distributed to EWM

Step 4: Distribute Inbound Delivery in EWM (Transaction: /N/SCWM/PRDI)

Post Goods Receipt

Step 5: Check Stock after GR in EWM (Transaction: MMBE)

Step 6: Check Inbound Delivery Document Flow (Transaction: VL33N)

Step 7: Warehouse Management Monitor (Transaction: /N/SCWM/MON)

Based on putaway strategy defined in EWM configuration (Refer Pre-Requisite Configuration steps), system automatically determines the storage type and storage bin details in background.

Step 8: Check Physical Stock post Warehouse Task Creation

Since Warehouse task not yet confirmed, stock still under GR-Zone 9010 Area (Refer warehouse process type step in Pre-Requisite configuration) 

Step 9: Confirm Warehouse Task

Step 10: Check Physical Stock in EWM after Warehouse Task Confirmation

click on refresh, stock updates under respective storage type and storage bin as per putaway strategy defined.

 

If you found this guide helpful, don't miss any other SAP MM WM blogs covering real-world automation, configurations tips, and best practices for procurement, inventory management, and warehouse management.

Click to view other SAP MM WM Blogs - SAP Community

 

Regards,

Mannem Leelamohan

entry.title: SAP EWM How to Create RF Performance Measurement
entry.content:
SAP EWM How to Create RF Performance Measurement

Introduction

Radio Frequency (RF) performance measurement is crucial for optimizing warehouse operations in SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM). While RF logging capabilities track user activities and system interactions, performance measurement specifically focuses on analyzing response times, throughput, and operational efficiency of RF transactions. This comprehensive guide addresses the implementation of RF performance measurement infrastructure as referenced in SAP, helping warehouse managers identify bottlenecks and optimize their RF-enabled processes.

What is RF Performance Measurement?

RF Performance Measurement in SAP EWM is a specialized monitoring framework designed to capture, analyze, and report on the performance metrics of radio frequency transactions and operations. Unlike standard RF logging which tracks user activities, performance measurement focuses on:

  • Response Time Analysis: Measuring backend processing times for RF transactions
  • End-to-End Performance Tracking: Complete user experience measurement from RF device interaction to system response
  • Throughput Optimization: Analyzing task completion rates and identifying process bottlenecks
  • Resource Utilization Monitoring: Tracking how efficiently RF resources are being utilized in warehouse operations

The framework provides warehouse managers with actionable insights to improve operational efficiency, reduce task completion times, and enhance overall warehouse productivity.

Activate the RF performance tool:
Execute transaction /SCWM/RSRC and activate the flag "RF performance measurement"

  1. Execute the RF transaction:
    Log in to RFUI and process a Warehouse Order (WO).
  2. Run the performance analysis:
    Execute transaction ST13 and enter PERF_TOOL and execute

Enter EWM_RF_ANALYSIS and execute.

On the next screen, press button "Details"

  1. Review the list of executed Function Modules and their runtime.

This tool highlights only the slow-performing Function Modules, allowing you to identify performance bottlenecks in RF processing and focus analysis on specific backend logic.

Be aware: The tool is designed to identify slow RF processing steps and does not trace every activity.

Prerequisites

Before implementing RF performance measures, ensure the following requirements are met:

System Requirements:

  • SAP EWM system with RF Framework activated
  • SAP S/4HANA 2020 or later versions for enhanced performance measurement capabilities
  • ITS Mobile service properly configured for RF device communication

Technical Prerequisites:

  • Performance Framework Module 2 (PFM2) infrastructure activated in the EWM system
  • ST-A/PI (Service Tools for Applications) version 01P or higher installed
  • Sufficient system resources allocated for performance data collection and storage

Authorization Requirements:

  • Administrative access to EWM configuration (SPRO)
  • Authorization for performance measurement transactions
  • Access to Warehouse Monitor (/SCWM/MON) for performance analysis

Step-by-Step Configuration Guide

Step 1: Access Performance Measurement Settings

Navigate to the performance measurement configuration through:

  • SPRO Path: Extended Warehouse Management → Settings → Resource Management → Performance Measurement
  • Transaction Code: Use /SCWM/RFLOGACT for RF-related performance settings
  • Alternative Access: Through Warehouse Monitor (/SCWM/MON) → Resource Management → Performance Analysis

Step 2: Define Performance Indicators

Configure key performance indicators (KPIs) for RF operations:

Standard Performance Metrics:

  • RF transaction response times
  • Task confirmation duration
  • Screen navigation efficiency
  • Validation processing times
  • Exception handling performance

Custom Performance Indicators:

  • Warehouse-specific metrics based on operational requirements
  • Resource group performance comparisons
  • Queue processing efficiency measurements

Step 3: Set Up Measurement Parameters

Configure measurement parameters through the performance framework:

Backend Runtime Measurement:

  • Activate function module execution time tracking
  • Configure threshold values for performance alerts
  • Set up data retention policies for performance records

Frontend Data Collection:

  • Implement optional Badi WORKLOAD_STATISTIC for enhanced metrics
  • Configure upload interface for frontend performance data
  • Define measurement overhead parameters to minimize system impact

Step 4: Configure RF Device Integration

Ensure proper integration between RF devices and performance measurement:

Device Configuration:

  • Verify RF device connectivity through ITS Mobile
  • Configure presentation profiles for performance-optimized screens
  • Set up resource assignments for performance tracking

Communication Settings:

  • Optimize HTTP/HTTPS communication parameters
  • Configure timeout values for performance measurement
  • Implement real-time data synchronization for accurate metrics

Step 5: Activate Performance Monitoring

Complete the activation process:

System Activation:

  • Execute performance measurement activation programs
  • Validate configuration through test transactions
  • Verify data collection is functioning correctly

Testing and Validation:

  • Perform end-to-end testing with actual RF devices
  • Validate performance data accuracy
  • Confirm reporting functionality is operational

Common Configuration Scenarios

High-Volume Warehouse Operations:

  • Configure parallel processing for performance data collection
  • Implement queue-specific performance monitoring
  • Set up automated performance threshold alerts

Multi-Resource Environment:

  • Define resource group-specific performance metrics
  • Configure comparative performance analysis between different resource types
  • Implement workload balancing based on performance data

Monitoring and Reporting

Available Analysis Tools:

  • Warehouse Monitor Integration: Performance metrics accessible through /SCWM/MON
  • Real-time Dashboards: Live performance monitoring capabilities
  • Historical Analysis: Trend analysis and performance pattern identification

Standard Reports:

  • RF transaction performance summaries
  • Resource utilization efficiency reports
  • Except for handling performance analysis
  • Comparative performance metrics across different time periods

Troubleshooting Common Issues

Performance Data Collection Issues:

  • Verify PFM2 infrastructure is properly activated
  • Check system resources allocation for performance measurement
  • Validate Badi implementations if custom performance metrics are used

RF Device Performance Problems:

  • Review of ITS Mobile configuration for optimal performance
  • Analyze network connectivity and response times
  • Optimize RF screen configurations for better performance

Data Analysis Challenges:

  • Ensure proper authorization for performance analysis transactions
  • Verify data retention settings are appropriate for analysis needs
  • Check system performance impact of measurement overhead

Best Practices

Configuration Recommendations:

  • Start with standard performance indicators before implementing custom metrics
  • Configure appropriate data retention periods to balance analysis needs with system performance
  • Implement gradual rollout across warehouse areas to validate configuration

Operational Guidelines:

  • Regular review of performance thresholds and alerts
  • Periodic analysis of performance trends to identify optimization opportunities
  • Integration with existing warehouse management processes and KPIs

Maintenance Considerations:

  • Schedule regular cleanup of performance data using standard cleanup programs
  • Monitor system impact of performance measurement and adjust parameters as needed
  • Keep performance measurement configuration aligned with warehouse operational changes

Conclusion

Implementing RF performance measurement in SAP EWM provides warehouse managers with powerful tools to optimize operations, identify bottlenecks, and improve overall efficiency. The framework enables data-driven decision making by providing detailed insights into RF transaction performance, resource utilization, and operational patterns. By following this comprehensive configuration guide, organizations can establish a robust performance measurement infrastructure that supports continuous improvement in warehouse operations.

The key benefits include enhanced operational visibility, proactive performance management, and the ability to optimize resource allocation based on actual performance data rather than assumptions.

Additional Resources

Related SAP Notes:

  • SAP Note 1595305: Measuring runtimes for RF devices in SAP EWM
  • SAP Note 1690850: Performance measurement in RFUI
  • SAP Note 69455: Service tools for Applications ST-A/PI
  • 3712387 - How to Create RF Performance Measurement in SAP EWM - SAP for Me

Hope that can help you.

Thank you and regards

Rogerio Lima.

entry.title: Start Implementing SAP EWM with this free course from SAP
entry.content:
What You’ll Learn in “Basic Customizing in SAP S/4HANA EWM”

If you’re planning to implement SAP EWM, this course is designed for practical application and  understanding the system environment to implementing key warehouse processes and basic integration with SAP ERP. In this blog I’ll take you through the structure of the course and how to expand on these skills.

 In the “Applying Basic Customizing in SAP S/4HANA EWM” course, you’ll learn to configure and apply essential implementation steps, including integrating SAP EWM with SAP ERP, creating product and packaging master data, defining warehouse structures and process types, implementing putaway and pick strategies, handling stock movements (postings, transfers, replenishment), and running basic physical inventory and post-processing tasks. The course does not cover advanced processes such as wave management.

Course Structure

The course contains 11 units and should take roughly six hours of focused lessons. Below is a breakdown:

  • Unit 1 — System environment
    Understand EWM’s place in the S/4HANA landscape: deployment options, architecture, and how embedded EWM differs from decentralized setups.
  • Unit 2 — Setting up integration
    Learn how to connect SAP ERP and EWM so that documents (deliveries, purchase orders) and master data flow correctly. Integration is foundational—without it, core inbound and outbound processes won’t function.

 

  • Unit 3 — Structural elements and master data
    Configure warehouses, storage types, sections, bins, product master data, and packaging specifications. Proper master data and structure ensure accurate stock visibility and predictable warehouse behavior.
  • Unit 4 — Warehouse process types
    Define standard process types used to trigger warehouse tasks and document flows. These are the building blocks for inbound and outbound logic.
  • Unit 5 — Receiving goods
    Covers goods receipt processes: direct and rule-based putaway, capacity checks, and posting goods receipts (GR) in embedded EWM. This unit shows how to get products into the right places efficiently.
  • Unit 6 — Shipping goods
    Focuses on outbound flows and picking strategies—critical for on-time delivery and accurate shipments.
  • Unit 7 — Storage control
    Introduces storage control configuration to map ERP warehouse tasks to physical activities and to control bin-level movements.
  • Unit 8 — Creating warehouse orders
    Learn how warehouse orders are created and managed—central for coordinating work across resources and tasks.
  • Unit 9 — Posting changes, stock transfers, replenishment
    Covers the mechanics of moving stock between types and statuses, posting corrections, and setting replenishment triggers—day-to-day operations you’ll configure and support.
  • Unit 10 — Physical inventory
    Covers configuration and execution of inventory counts and adjustments—essential for auditability and accurate stock.
  • Unit 11 — Post Processing Framework
    Provides an overview of PPF to automate follow-up actions (notifications, status changes) after key events.

Why these skills matter in practice

1. Faster, smoother go-lives: solid basics reduce rework during detailed configuration and testing.
2. Fewer operational exceptions: well-defined putaway/pick rules and capacity checks minimize manual interventions.
3. Better integration: a correct ERP–EWM setup reduces document mismatches and stock inconsistencies.

 

Tips to get the most from the course
  • Practice in a sandbox system: configuration concepts become clear when you try them.
  • Map course concepts to your warehouse processes: draw current vs. target flows to see where settings apply

After completing the basic course, you can advance to topics such as labor and resource management, wave and yard management, automation, and more extensive integration within the Business Suite. Links to these courses can be found here.

Wrapping Up

Completing the SAP EWM basic course gives you a solid foundation in warehouse management fundamentals, practical configuration skills, and hands-on processes you can apply on a project. With this base, you’ll be better equipped to set up SAP EWM basics. Whether you’re upskilling for implementation skills or preparing your team for digital transformation, this course is a practical first step toward greater efficiency and control in your warehouse operations. In my next blog, I’ll cover advanced topics and real-world implementation tips.

For more information on SAP S/4HANA EWM, see the community page set up by the product management team at SAP: https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/extended-warehouse-management

Here is a list of informative implementation guides for SAP S/4HANA EWM: https://dam.sap.com/mac/app/p/pdf/asset/preview/hv2DfQT?ltr=a&rc=10&doi=SAP894096

entry.title: SAP EWM License Audit Tools
entry.content:
SAP EWM License Audit Tools

Introduction

Managing SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) licenses across complex enterprise landscapes has always been challenging, particularly when dealing with decentralized warehouse operations. Organizations often struggle to maintain clear visibility into their license compliance across distributed EWM environments, leading to potential audit risks and unexpected costs.

SAP has now enhanced the self-auditing report /SCWM/RP_LICENCE_SELF_AUDIT to provide better transparency for decentralized EWM systems. This enhancement addresses a critical gap in license tracking for decentralized environments, ensuring organizations can confidently monitor their EWM usage patterns and maintain compliance with their licensing agreements.

The enhancement eliminates guesswork in license management and provides the clarity needed for confident compliance monitoring across both centralized and decentralized warehouse operations.

What's New: Enhanced License Self-Auditing

The enhanced /SCWM/RP_LICENCE_SELF_AUDIT report now provides specialized handling for decentralized EWM systems with a key improvement: clear identification through a 'DECNTRAL' identifier in the 'Actual Usage' field. This enhancement transforms how organizations track and manage licenses across their EWM landscape.

You can use this report to select and review a list of tracked warehouse numbers based on their usage of basic or advanced functions, monitoring compliance throughout the last 12 months. The report provides comprehensive visibility into delivery items per working day, total delivery items per warehouse, and compliance status across your entire EWM environment,

The benefits extend beyond simple identification – organizations now have unified visibility into license compliance across both centralized and decentralized warehouse operations, streamlining audit processes and reducing compliance risks.

Understanding Decentralized EWM Systems

Key Characteristics

Decentralized EWM represents a deployment model where Extended Warehouse Management runs on a separate system from SAP S/4HANA while maintaining full integration with the core solution for master data and transactional data, this architecture provides enhanced performance levels and ensures warehouse business process continuity during system maintenance or downtime.

Unlike embedded EWM, which shares the same client as SAP S/4HANA, decentralized EWM operates independently while seamlessly communicating with the enterprise management system for critical business processes. This separation allows for dedicated warehouse processing power and improved system resilience.

License Classification

A fundamental principle of decentralized EWM licensing is that these systems are always considered Advanced Warehouse Management. This classification is automatic and non-negotiable – there is no "basic" license option for decentralized deployments.

This Advanced classification becomes transparent in the 'Acknowledged Usage of Warehouse Management' field, ensuring complete visibility into the licensing implications of choosing a decentralized architecture. Organizations planning decentralized EWM implementations must budget for Advanced licenses from the outset, as the license type differentiation for Basic or Advanced is only applicable for embedded deployment cases,

How to Use Enhanced Report

Step-by-Step Process

  1. Navigate to Transaction: Access /SCWM/RP_LICENCE_SELF_AUDIT through transaction SE38 or directly via the transaction code

  1. Configure Selection Parameters: Enter specific warehouse numbers or client details, or leave blank to review all warehouses in the system
  2. Execute the Report: Run the analysis to generate comprehensive usage and compliance data
  3. Review Results: Examine the output for compliance status, usage patterns, and system identification

Reading the Results

The enhanced report now clearly displays 'DECNTRAL' in the 'Actual Usage' field for decentralized systems. This immediate identification helps distinguish system types during audit reviews. The report provides monthly breakdowns of Basic and Advanced feature usage, including total delivery items per month, average items per working day, and compliance status from both usage and customization perspectives,

For decentralized systems, the 'Acknowledged Usage of Warehouse Management' field will consistently show Advanced Warehouse Management, reflecting the mandatory license classification. The report tracks compliance over the last 12 months, providing historical visibility for trend analysis and audit preparation.

Important Limitations to Know

Supported Reports on Embedded Systems

While the self-auditing report has been enhanced, two other license-related reports remain unsupported in decentralized EWM environments: /SCWM/RP_COMPLIANCE_CHECK and /SCWM/RP_LICENCE_ANALYSIS, attempting to execute these reports in decentralized systems will generate error messages.

The good news is that SAP has improved these error messages to make the limitation transparent, reducing confusion for users who might attempt to run unsupported reports. The error message "/SCWM/TM033" now clearly indicates that the transaction or program is not supported for decentralized systems.

For Embedded System use the report /SCWM/RP_COMPLIANCE_CHECK and /SCWM/RP_LICENCE_ANALYSIS

 

Report /SCWM/RP_LICENCE_ANALYSIS 

Best Practice: Stick to Self-Auditing

For decentralized systems, /SCWM/RP_LICENCE_SELF_AUDIT is the only supported license auditing report. This consolidation simplifies the compliance process by providing a single, comprehensive tool for license management in decentralized environments.

Organizations should establish processes that rely exclusively on the self-auditing report for decentralized EWM license compliance, avoiding attempts to use the unsupported alternatives. This approach ensures consistent and reliable license tracking across the entire EWM landscape.

Business Impact and Benefits

For License Managers

Enhanced reporting provides unprecedented visibility into decentralized system usage patterns, enabling more accurate license planning and compliance monitoring. License managers can now easily identify which systems are decentralized and understand their Advanced license requirements briefly.

The 12-month historical view helps with trend analysis and future license planning, while the clear compliance indicators reduce audit preparation time and effort. This transparency eliminates the risk of unexpected license compliance issues during audits.

For IT Teams

Technical teams benefit from clearer error messaging when attempting to use unsupported reports, reducing troubleshooting time and confusion. The enhanced self-auditing report provides all necessary technical details for system administration and compliance verification.

The standardization on a single reporting tool for decentralized systems simplifies training requirements and reduces the complexity of license management procedures. IT teams can focus on maintaining compliance through one reliable reporting mechanism.

Implementation Considerations

Organizations using decentralized EWM should ensure they have the latest version of the self-auditing report, which may require implementing SAP Note 3712523. The enhancement is particularly relevant for organizations running decentralized EWM on S/4HANA or classic EWM systems.

Integration with existing license management processes should account for the automatic Advanced license classification of decentralized systems. Training programs should emphasize the limitations of other license reports in decentralized environments and focus on the enhanced self-auditing capabilities.

Regular execution of the self-auditing report should become part of standard compliance procedures, with particular attention to the new 'DECNTRAL' identifiers and their implications for license management. Organizations should establish monthly or quarterly review cycles to maintain optimal compliance visibility.

Conclusion

The enhanced EWM license self-auditing functionality represents a significant step forward in managing license compliance for decentralized warehouse operations. By providing clear identification of decentralized systems and their Advanced license requirements, organizations can maintain better control over their EWM licensing landscape.

For organizations operating or planning decentralized EWM implementations, this enhancement eliminates ambiguity and provides the transparency needed for confident license management. The consolidation of license auditing into a single, comprehensive report simplifies compliance processes while ensuring complete visibility into system usage patterns.

We recommend that all organizations with decentralized EWM systems implement this enhancement and establish regular self-auditing procedures to maintain optimal license compliance and avoid potential retrospective penalties.

Additional Resources

  • SAP Note 3712523: Enhanced Self Auditing Report for decentralized systems
  • SAP Note 3392135: Self Audit Report implementation
  • SAP Note 3699217: Error handling for unsupported reports in decentralized systems
  • Transaction /SCWM/RP_LICENCE_SELF_AUDIT: Self-auditing report execution
  • SAP Help Portal: Extended Warehouse Management documentation

Hope that can help you.

Thank you and regards

Rogerio Lima.

entry.title: SAP Extended Warehouse Management for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition, 2025 FPS01 – What’s New
entry.content:
We have recently released the latest updates for SAP Extended Warehouse Management for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition, 2025 FPS01. With this release, we further expand the Predictive Labor Demand Planning capabilities available for SAP EWM, add in more functionalities and scenarios for the Advanced Shipping & Receiving integration, deliver a host of extensibility and user experience improvements, strengthen our Suite Integration capabilities, and much more.

And unlike many of our past product release blog posts, I would like to begin today’s overview with the latest updates to our AI capabilities in Extended Warehouse Management. To find out more technical details and information on each feature (such as Customization steps) I shall link the corresponding “What’s New” viewer from the help portal in the title of the subchapters.

ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE

With our AI strategy for Logistics and warehousing, we aim to address further on the labor and resource shortage in the warehouse execution. We achieve this via machine learning–based predictive analytics to forecast current and future workloads and conduct forecast based on long-term historical data across warehouse operations, as well as via Joule copilot to provide advanced warehouse operation, and proposing insightful solutions to streamline the outbound process.
